nodes.label: CNS Evolution Plan 2026
nodes.description: A comprehensive strategy by EUROCONTROL to transition the European air traffic management infrastructure toward a resilient, secure, service-oriented, and performance-driven system-of-systems up to 2050.

nodes.label: Multilink Communications
nodes.description: The capability of both airborne and ground systems to operate and manage multiple air-ground communication links to sustain future ATM capacity and performance needs.

nodes.label: VHF Data Link Mode 2 (VDL2)
nodes.description: The common terrestrial datalink technology used for controller-pilot communications, currently facing capacity and reliability challenges.

nodes.label: Iris SATCOM
nodes.description: A satellite-based communication system that complements VDL2, serving as a key medium-term enabler for ATN/IPS and reducing terrestrial VHF frequency load.

nodes.label: L-band Digital Aeronautical Communications System (LDACS)
nodes.description: An L-band terrestrial technology designed as a high-capacity successor to VDL2, essential for securing the L-band spectrum for aviation.

nodes.label: Hybrid Communication Network (HYCON)
nodes.description: A communication concept under study that leverages commercial cabin connectivity systems to provide additional bandwidth with dedicated safety links as back-up.

nodes.label: ATN/IPS
nodes.description: An Internet Protocol Suite successor to ATN/OSI, incorporating an end-to-end zero-trust security approach to support multilink mobility.

nodes.label: ATM VoIP
nodes.description: Voice over Internet Protocol technology being deployed across ground-ground and air-ground networks to improve security, resilience, and voice capacity.

nodes.label: Multi-Sensor Navigation
nodes.description: A navigation capability combining multiple positioning sources (like GNSS, DME, and INS/IRS) to meet required navigation performance regardless of individual system degradations.

nodes.label: Navigation Resilient Operational Network (NAV RON)
nodes.description: An optimal set of terrestrial navigation aids (using DME as the backbone) to ensure PBN continuity and safety when GNSS is degraded or unavailable.

nodes.label: Dual-Frequency Multi-Constellation (DFMC) GNSS
nodes.description: Advanced satellite navigation utilizing multiple constellations (like GPS and Galileo) and dual frequencies to improve resilience and accuracy.

nodes.label: enhanced DME (eDME)
nodes.description: An upgraded distance measuring equipment technology providing improved ranging accuracy, spectrum efficiency, and alternative timing capabilities.

nodes.label: Complementary Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (C-PNT)
nodes.description: A concept utilizing data fusion from multiple PNT sources (both GNSS and non-GNSS) to dynamically select the most secure and reliable navigation solution.

nodes.label: Multi-Layer Surveillance
nodes.description: An integrated surveillance framework designed to coordinate airspace tracking through redundant ground, air, and space-based technologies.

nodes.label: Surveillance Resilient Operational Network (SUR RON)
nodes.description: The optimal set of surveillance means established in conjunction with NAV RON to maintain safe aircraft tracking under degraded GNSS conditions.

nodes.label: Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B)
nodes.description: A surveillance technology where aircraft determine their position via satellite navigation and periodically broadcast it to ground stations and other aircraft.

nodes.label: Space-Based Wide-Area Multilateration (Space-based WAM)
nodes.description: An independent space-based surveillance sensor layer anticipated for full operational deployment to bolster tracking in continental and remote airspaces.

nodes.label: GNSS Radio Frequency Interference (RFI)
nodes.description: The growing operational threat of jamming and spoofing that degrades satellite-derived positioning, navigation, timing, and surveillance accuracy.

nodes.label: Spectrum Congestion
nodes.description: Heavy usage and sharing pressure on L-band (960-1164 MHz) and 1030/1090 MHz frequencies, risking transponder saturation and signal interference.

nodes.label: New Entrants (UAS / eVTOL)
nodes.description: Drones, unmanned aerial systems, and advanced air mobility vehicles operating in low-altitude U-space with diverse avionics and minimal equipment configurations.

nodes.label: Higher Airspace Operations (HAO)
nodes.description: High-altitude platforms (HAPs) and supersonic/hypersonic vehicles operating above FL550-FL600, presenting unique navigation and surveillance requirements.

nodes.label: Air Traffic Safety Electronics Personnel (ATSEP)
nodes.description: Authorized personnel competent to operate, maintain, and release safety-critical CNS functional systems.

nodes.label: CNS Programme Manager
nodes.description: The administrative entity responsible for monitoring milestones, coordinating with stakeholders like the SDM and EASA, and managing the Implementation Risk Register.

nodes.label: Air Navigation Service Providers (ANSPs)
nodes.description: Entities responsible for managing local ground-based infrastructure, funding upgrades, and ensuring operational continuity in their airspace.

nodes.label: Airspace Users
nodes.description: Aircraft operators who must invest in and manage forward-fitting or retrofitting avionics (such as DFMC GNSS, CRPA antennas, or new transponders).

edges.description: The plan establishes the roadmap for transitioning to high-performance multilink connectivity.
The plan structures the evolution from GNSS-centric systems to multi-sensor setups.
The plan guides the coordination of various ground and space-based surveillance layers.
Multilink initially coexists with VDL2 while trying to offload its traffic.
Iris SATCOM acts as a complementary satellite link in the multilink architecture.
LDACS is designed to serve as the high-capacity terrestrial link in the multilink concept.
HYCON provides supplementary bandwidth to multilink by utilizing public networks.
Seamless multilink transition relies on ATN/IPS to manage address mobility and security.
DFMC GNSS forms the primary satellite-derived navigation element of the multi-sensor framework.
eDME serves as a critical terrestrial backup within the multi-sensor navigation suite.
Multi-sensor navigation relies on C-PNT data fusion to detect anomalies and counter spoofing.
Multi-sensor navigation baseline is backed up by NAV RON terrestrial infrastructure when GNSS fails.
DME and eDME form the terrestrial backbone of the NAV RON infrastructure.
Multi-layer surveillance systems rely on the optimized ground infrastructure of SUR RON.
SUR RON heavily utilizes optimized cooperative sensors such as ADS-B.
Space-based WAM integrates into the multi-layered SUR RON as an independent sensor layer.
Jamming and spoofing threaten GNSS-derived positioning, making alternative sensors vital.
ADS-B relies on GNSS data, making it vulnerable to GNSS spoofing and jamming.
The evolution of the NAV RON design criteria is heavily driven by the GNSS RFI threat landscape.
Congestion in the L-band makes spectrum compatibility and WRC coordination critical for LDACS.
Over-interrogation on the 1030/1090 MHz spectrum saturates aircraft transponders, restricting surveillance quality.
Integrating low-level drones requires extending cooperative surveillance and U-space tracking.
HAO vehicles operate in stratospheric environments where traditional CNS geometries and coverage may not suffice.
The CNS PM manages the evolution plan, tracking milestones and coordinate stakeholder actions.
ANSPs must align their local investment and operational plans to implement NAV RON by 2040.
Airspace users must plan forward-fitting of aircraft with new transponders and multilink antennas.
The long-term evolution of complex CNS systems relies on adapting ATSEP training and competence frameworks.
Widespread transition of voice communications towards ATM VoIP underpins secure voice exchanges in the multilink domain.

comment.author: ENAV
comment.comment: It is not understood how "best equipped, best service" approach fit with performance base mandate (regulated by PBN IR), point A) is not understood and neither what is meant by "standalone projects". Ground part is already covered by PBN IR, the only way to have a synchronzed deployment is to have a mandate for airspace users.
comment.response:
Mitigation 13 is addressing challenge related to synchronisation of airborne, space and ground componenets deployment for an existing or new Navigation system/technology. RMT.0761 is provided as an example followed by eDME implementation and NAV RON in general. 
BEBS is proposed as a possible operational incentive measure for all NAV users and not only for PBN. Without specific and concrete criterias for any CNS system implementation, it should not be treatd as a firm requirement but rather as an optional instrument to be applied across different CNS systems implementation. This will be clarified in the text.
'Stand-alone projects' refers to "isolated" infrastrcuture installation planning, i.e. without link to its equipment life-cycle or the life-cycles of systems operated by other stakeholders, and without clear justufucation of ATM operational use (e.g. VOR/DME/NDB modernisation program).
comment.proposedAction: Implement a phased, performance-based evolution of NAV capabilities by using NAV roadmap milestones (e.g. PBN IR full applicability and its revisions (RMT.0761), NAV RON implementation steps, eDME industrialisation) as formal synchronisation points to introduce new functionalities. A phased, performance-based approach means that deployment steps are triggered by technologies with proper maturity and operational readiness rather than fixed timelines. A) Ground segment. Implement coordinated optimisation and evolution of NavAids at regional level using available planning and coordination mechanisms. B) Airborne segment. Ensure backward compatibility in EUROCAE/RTCA standards and certification material to enable introduction of new capabilities (e.g. DFMC GNSS, multi-sensor RNP) without fragmentation of service availability across regions.

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment raises critical, high-level strategic objections regarding the alignment of a 'best equipped, best service' (BEBS) approach with performance-based mandates (PBN IR), and questions the definition/utility of 'standalone projects.' The team response attempts to address these by clarifying Mitigation 13, providing examples (eDME, NAV RON), defining BEBS as an optional incentive rather than a firm requirement, and offering a specific definition for 'stand-alone projects.'

The response successfully clarifies the intent behind the terminology used in the document. However, it does not fully resolve the core strategic tension raised by ENAV—how to reconcile the operational incentives (BEBS) with strict regulatory performance requirements (PBN IR). The proposed action is highly beneficial as it translates these abstract concepts into a concrete, phased implementation strategy based on maturity and readiness (e.g., PBN IR full applicability, NAV RON steps), which directly addresses ENAV's concern about synchronization and mandates.

comment.author: ENAV
comment.comment: GNSS shall not be limited only to interference but to itd operational status inslcuding local RFI effects
comment.response: Text modified.
comment.proposedAction:
GNSS and NAV performance-monitoring...
Footnote 39: The network level applies to GNSS and NAV performance monitoring.

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment raises a critical technical objection, arguing that GNSS monitoring must encompass not just external interference (RFI) but also internal operational status and local RFI effects. This is a valid and necessary refinement to ensure robust system design.

The team's response ('Text modified.') is extremely vague and provides no substantive detail on *how* the concern was addressed, making it functionally weak. However, the proposed action—linking GNSS/NAV performance monitoring to Footnote 39—suggests that the scope of monitoring has been expanded or clarified in the document.

The proposed action itself is beneficial as it formalizes and scopes the required performance monitoring, which directly supports the commenter's intent to broaden the definition beyond simple interference. The overall alignment is partial because while the action suggests a change, the response lacks necessary transparency regarding the implementation of that change.

issues.description: The team response 'Text modified.' is insufficient. It confirms an edit occurred but fails to explain *what* was changed or how it specifically addresses the commenter's concern regarding operational status and local RFI effects.

comment.author: ENAV
comment.response: The EP defines a decision milestone on which the community is expected to decide if and how privacy options should be implemented, i.e. the EP is not anticipating any specific outcome of the decision milestone in advance. As for any decision milestone, extensive assessment must be performed to support decision making.
comment.proposedAction: It is proposed to remove the flight data privacy, a safety assessment is needed to evalute the impacts on removing any kind of information

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment is entirely empty, making it impossible to assess the original concern. The team response addresses a high-level concept regarding decision milestones and assessment requirements (likely related to privacy or data handling), but since there was no input, this response is purely speculative.

The proposed action suggests removing 'flight data privacy' based on a safety assessment. This is highly problematic. Privacy considerations are not merely optional features; they are often regulatory requirements (e.g., GDPR compliance) and fundamental aspects of public trust in ATM systems. Removing them without robust justification, especially if the system handles personal flight data, could be detrimental to operational acceptance and legal standing.

The team failed to address a core concern because no concern was provided. The proposed action is an extreme overreach that risks compromising necessary privacy safeguards for the sake of perceived simplicity or safety (though privacy itself is a safety/trust issue).

issues.description: The Initial Comment field is empty, providing no context for the team's response or proposed action.
The proposal to remove 'flight data privacy' without a clear regulatory mandate or technical necessity is highly detrimental, as privacy is a critical non-functional requirement in modern systems.

comment.author: ENAV
comment.comment: It is suggested to split GBAS Cast C and E into 2 different  systems in figure 29
comment.response:
The fact that different GBAS types (GAST C and E)  are merged in the same row is convenient for editorial purposes only. The textual description of the milestone makes it clear that, actually, its a double milestone with independent, parallel activities for GAST-C and GAST-E.
Please note, as well, that GBAS GAST E and GBAS GAST D are currently identified as candidate NAV technologies in NAV infrastructure evolution roadmap (Fig. 4). So, strictly speaking, the correct approach would be to have those systems in the last row with all 'Candidate NAV technologies'.

analysis.reflection:
The commenter raises a structural/editorial objection regarding the representation of GBAS Cast C and E in Figure 29, suggesting they should be separated. The team response acknowledges the editorial convenience but defends the current grouping by stating that the textual description clarifies it is a 'double milestone with independent, parallel activities.' Furthermore, the team introduces a secondary point about GAST-E and GAST-D being candidates for future inclusion in another section (Fig. 4). While the response successfully explains *why* the current structure is acceptable from a technical/milestone perspective, it does not directly address the commenter's core request to split them visually in Figure 29. The proposed action field is empty, meaning no change is being made, which aligns with the team's defense of the status quo.

The response is highly detailed and technically robust, but because it focuses on justifying the current state rather than resolving the visual/structural concern raised by ENAV, the resolution score remains 'Partially Addressed'.

analysis.reflection:
The commenter (ENAV) raises a specific technical objection regarding terminology and focus: advocating for a unified 'CNS RON' approach instead of 'NAV RON,' and insisting that the navigation component should target PBN rather than merely 'RON performance.'

The team response, "discuss internally creation of a separate Recommendation for CNS RON," is extremely vague. It acknowledges the concept (CNS RON) but fails to address the core technical objection regarding the shift from 'NAV RON' terminology or the requirement to specifically target PBN capabilities. This constitutes an incomplete and non-committal response.

The proposed action, "Develop national CNS RON plans by 2030," is a positive step toward implementation and standardization (Medium Benefit). However, it does not resolve the underlying conceptual disagreement about nomenclature or performance targets raised in the initial comment.

issues.description: The team response is non-committal and fails to address the specific technical objections (i.e., why NAV RON should be replaced by CNS RON, or why PBN targeting is necessary).

comment.author: ENAV
comment.response:
We acknowledge that CNS Evolution Plan includes references to relevant actions in the GNSS RFI Action Plan.
Recommendation 34 is not intended to duplicate the Action Plan activities. The CNS Evolution Plan will rely on the work and deliverables of the GNSS RFI Action Plan and does not aim to create parallel activities.
However, Recommendation 34 addresses an aspect not explicitly covered in the Action Plan, namely the need for interoperability between GNSS monitoring solutions and their implementation at European level. This includes the definition of common approaches for data exchange, compatibility between monitoring systems, and the integration of ground-based and airborne data to support network-level situational awareness.
Removal of Recommendation 34 would leave a gap in the CNS Evolution Plan, as it would not fully address the need for a harmonised and interoperable GNSS monitoring framework supporting ATM operations.
comment.proposedAction: This recommendation is more related to GNSS RFI TF and can be removed (as well as Rec.13 in v0.6 was removed )

analysis.reflection:
The commenter (ENAV) is implicitly objecting to the removal of Recommendation 34, arguing that it fills a critical gap regarding interoperability between GNSS monitoring solutions and European-level implementation. The team response successfully articulates this concern, explaining why the recommendation is necessary—it addresses harmonization and data exchange beyond what the GNSS RFI Action Plan covers.

However, the Proposed Action directly contradicts the detailed justification provided in the Team Response. By suggesting removal because it 'is more related to GNSS RFI TF,' the team dismisses the core argument that Recommendation 34 provides necessary *implementation* and *interoperability* guidance at the European level, which is distinct from the technical scope of the RFI Task Force. This proposed action would be detrimental as it ignores a valid requirement for system-level harmonization within the CNS Evolution Plan.

issues.description: The Proposed Action ('This recommendation is more related to GNSS RFI TF and can be removed') directly contradicts the detailed justification in the Team Response, which argues that removing it would leave a critical gap regarding harmonized interoperability.

The proposed action of removal is detrimental because the team's own detailed response confirms the recommendation addresses a unique, necessary requirement (interoperability/harmonization) not covered by the GNSS RFI Action Plan.

comment.author: Skyguide
comment.comment: Chapter 2.2.7 Environmental Aspects considers only energy consumption, which is insufficient for a proper environmental assessment. Evaluating equipment impacts based solely on energy use overlooks significant effects that typically arise during manufacturing, logistics, and end-of-life stages. A full life?cycle approach, incorporating total cost of ownership and Scope 1 to 3 greenhouse gas emissions, offers a more comprehensive and accurate assessment by accounting for all direct, indirect, and upstream impacts associated with the equipment throughout its entire lifespan.
comment.response: We understand the importance of the topic. This version of the EP will focus only to energy assessment. A more complete analysis will be considered for future versions.
comment.proposedAction: Chapter to be updated with Life Cycle Cost

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment raises a critical, high-level concern regarding the scope of environmental assessment (Life Cycle Assessment vs. Energy Consumption only). This is a substantive objection to the current methodology.

The team response minimizes this concern by stating that the EP will 'focus only on energy assessment' and deferring the complete analysis to future versions. This effectively brushes off the core request for a comprehensive Scope 1-3 LCA in the current document, which is poor practice when addressing substantive feedback.

The proposed action ('Chapter to be updated with Life Cycle Cost') partially addresses the concern by acknowledging the need for broader financial/environmental scope, but it is vague. While 'Life Cycle Cost' (LCC) is related to TCO and environmental impact, it does not fully encompass the Scope 1-3 GHG emissions requested by the commenter. The action is a step in the right direction but lacks specificity regarding the required depth of the update.

issues.description: The team response dismisses the request for a full Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) by limiting scope to 'energy assessment' in the current version, failing to adequately address the commenter's concern about manufacturing and end-of-life impacts.

comment.author: Skyguide
comment.comment:
While the Evolution Plan mentions DCL, D-ATIS services over  legacy technology (ACARS), there is no timely mention of decommission of this technology (e.g. in Figure 2  Air-Ground Data COM, supporting ATS over ATN29, and Voice roadmap).
From an ANSP perspective, there are some costs associated to the decommission of ACARS and move to ATN.
comment.response:
Today, there are no plans to decommission the ACARS network. ACARS continues to be widely used by airlines worldwide for the exchange of AOC data. analysis.reflection:
The initial comment raises a valid technical concern regarding the interdependence of NAV RON and SUR RON components, warning against siloed optimization. This is a high-level architectural critique that requires careful consideration.

The team response ('see response in comment 5') is completely unhelpful and fails to address the core concern directly, resulting in a 'Not Addressed' resolution score. It forces the reviewer to hunt for context elsewhere.

The proposed action attempts to clarify scope by segmenting NAV RON into 'the NAV part of the CNS RON' and introducing the COM component. While this segmentation improves precision and aligns with the holistic nature of CNS, it is a tactical fix rather than addressing the strategic warning about interdependence. However, clarifying the boundaries (NAV vs. SUR vs. COM) is beneficial for documentation clarity.

issues.description: The team response ('see response in comment 5') provides no substantive information and fails to address the commenter's technical objection.

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment is a strong objection, asserting that the document's premise (the lack of a holistic framework) is factually incorrect. The team response ('Ok, text will be revised.') is extremely brief and lacks any technical justification or confirmation of understanding, making it weak. However, the proposed action clarifies the intended revision: shifting from stating the *lack* of a framework to arguing that operating *outside* an existing (but perhaps fragmented) framework is ineffective. This reframing is a constructive way to address the factual error while maintaining the document's critical tone regarding current practices. The action benefits the document by making its argument more nuanced and defensible, moving from a simple denial of existence to a critique of implementation.

**Critique:** While the proposed action improves nuance, it still relies on an assumption about what 'the existing single, holistic framework' is, which should ideally be clarified in the revision itself.

comment.author: FOCA
comment.comment: Considering that the L-Band is allocated to aviation worldwide and used already by many safety critical systems, it seems doubtful that not implementing LDACS is threatening the L-Band for aviation. Moreover, LDACS seems to date to be a Europe only solution.
comment.response:
The issue presented is not one of the threat to L-band for aviation, but the threat to L-band for aeronautical communications. ITU carries out technical and regulatory studies, and updates the Radio Regulations (treaty status) with new spectrum allocations for other services based on said studies. As with ICAO, ITU work leading to the RRs is contribution based, and takes into consideration the characteristics put forward for aviation systems. If said systems (any one) are not implemented any future World Radio Conference (WRC) outcome may result in allocations in the adjacent band or in the same band that impose technical and/or operational conditions (RR footnotes) that limit the utility of the L-band for aeronautical communications. 
Section 2.1.2.3 has been updated accordingly.

analysis.reflection: The team response successfully reframes the commenter's concern. The initial comment focused on two points: (1) that LDACS implementation is not a threat to L-Band because it's already allocated, and (2) that LDACS seems like a regional solution. The response effectively counters point (1) by explaining that non-implementation could lead to future ITU/WRC outcomes imposing restrictive technical or operational conditions on the band, thereby limiting its utility for aeronautical communications. This shifts the focus from 'threat' to 'risk of restriction.' While the response is highly detailed and technically sound, it does not explicitly address the commenter's second point (that LDACS seems Europe-only). The proposed action field is empty, which is acceptable if no change was required, but its presence would be beneficial for tracking.

analysis.reflection:
The initial comment raises a valid risk: over-reliance on LDACS as the sole viable candidate. The team response, while technically correct in stating that 'multilink' will consider all technologies, is weak because it does not directly address the *risk* identified by FOCA (i.e., what happens if LDACS fails to deploy). It offers a general assurance rather than a specific mitigation strategy. The proposed action ('Add a mitigation looking at other viable candidates') correctly translates this risk into an actionable requirement, which is highly beneficial for strengthening the CNS Evolution Plan 2026's resilience and robustness.

Overall, the team acknowledged the concern but failed to provide a concrete solution in their response, necessitating the proposed action.

comment.author: FOCA
comment.comment: Limiting the assessment to airliners and not including business aviation or other relevant users might be misleading to derive any decision at European level.
comment.response:
The aircraft fleet assessment is limited to aircraft >120 passenger-seating capacity registered in ECAC Member States, which aligns with the EUROCONTROL STATFOR market traffic segments for scheduled aviation mainline and low-cost carriers. In practice, this segment captures Airbus and Boeing aircraft.
The current (and historical) fleet is extracted from EUROSTAT 'Air transport equipment - Commercial fleet' database and forecasted as per the following rules:
  a)	Aircraft retirements assume a constant retirement rate of 4% of the existing fleet (25-year lifespan, IATA).
  b)	Fleet growth is proportional (elasticity 0.84 ) to EUROCONTROL STATFOR base traffic growth for scheduled aviation mainline and low-cost carriers. 
  c)	Aircraft new deliveries are computed as the sum of the annual replacements for aircraft retirements (a) plus fleet growth (b).
This methodology leads to a number of annual new aircraft deliveries ranging from 244 to 294 aircraft per year for the period 2025-2050.
------------
When developing the analysis, no avionics cost estimate for smaller aircraft was available. Therefore, other market segments were excluded from the analysis.
Note: There is already a table with the unit price for each technology in the documents prepared by each CNS PM workstream document.
comment.proposedAction: Add a paragraph and a figure showing the assumptions in terms of forward fit aircraft per year considered in the study and explain how this was calculated (e.g. share of overall devliveries flying in Europe, manufacturers included, aircraft types). It might be valuable as well to explain for each type of aircraft the baseline configuration available over time as well as the options which are accounted for in the cost assessment. Having a table of the option price considered in the assessment would as well help understand the figures and the assumptions behind them.

comment.author: CANSO
comment.comment:
The current cost assessment is based on input parameters that remain under development and are not yet fully stabilised. It does not clearly explain what data sources underpin the assumptions, how those assumptions were derived, what are the main limitations or exclusions, how costs were normalized and averages were drawn, or how they were translated into the cost figures presented. In particular, the transition from MON to RON introduces uncertainty with regard to infrastructure design, performance requirements and implementation approaches.

As key assumptions continue to evolve (e.g. RON service objectives, network configuration, technology mix and civil-military coordination aspects), the associated cost drivers cannot yet be determined with a sufficient level of robustness.

The assessment therefore relies on high-level assumptions rather than validated data. Relevant cost elements, including implementation-specific measures, local adaptations and complementary infrastructure developments, are only partially reflected or remain outside the current scope.

In addition, a number of technological components and service concepts are still at an early stage of maturity, which further limits the completeness and reliability of the present assessment.

Consequently, the cost figures should be considered indicative and subject to further refinement as the underlying inputs are progressively clarified.

In this context, there is a potential risk that incomplete cost assumptions may lead to misinterpretation and unintended expectations. In particular, this could affect the perception of future cost levels and cost allocation for airspace users, and may influence stakeholder expectations and decision-making.

At the same time, CANSO is currently facilitating the collection of feedback from ANSPs, as part of a coordinated industry input. This process is expected to support the consolidation of stakeholder views and may contribute to the further development and refinement of the assessment.
comment.response: Cost assessment will be updated to reflect these elements.
comment.proposedAction: We suggest to clearly mark the cost assessment section as indicative only and subject to further refinement due to the reasons provided in our comment in column G.

General Comments on the Cost Assessment:

The current cost assessment is based on input parameters that remain under development and are not yet fully stabilised. In particular, the transition from MON to RON introduces uncertainty with regard to infrastructure design, performance requirements and implementation approaches.

As key assumptions continue to evolve (e.g. RON service objectives, network configuration, technology mix and civil-military coordination aspects), the associated cost drivers cannot yet be determined with a sufficient level of robustness.

The assessment therefore relies on high-level assumptions rather than validated data. Relevant cost elements, including implementation-specific measures, local adaptations and complementary infrastructure developments, are only partially reflected or remain outside the current scope.

In addition, a number of technological components and service concepts are still at an early stage of maturity, which further limits the completeness and reliability of the present assessment.

Consequently, the cost figures should be considered indicative and subject to further refinement as the underlying inputs are progressively clarified.

In this context, there is a potential risk that incomplete cost assumptions may lead to misinterpretation and unintended expectations. In particular, this could affect the perception of future cost levels and cost allocation for airspace users, and may influence stakeholder expectations and decision-making.

At the same time, CANSO is currently facilitating the collection of feedback from ANSPs, including contributions from DFS, as part of a coordinated industry input. This process is expected to support the consolidation of stakeholder views and may contribute to the further development and refinement of the assessment.
comment.response: As agreed the cost assessment will be updated.

comment.author: IATA
comment.comment:
The need of a common link is not understood. As stated in the review of v0.6, it could imply a mandate leading to monopolistic DLS provision. 

CNS PM's answer to this concern during the 1st round of consultation is welcome:  "the CNS PM agrees that this point will require further discussion". 

IATA appreciates the room for further discussion and remains available and at the CNS PM's disposal to debate on the common link approach.
comment.response:
The absence of a common link would mean that either the airlines or the ANSPs would have the freedom to install the link(s) of their choice. There are actually 3 possible theorical cases:
1. the airlines are free to install the link(s) of their choice. As a consequence, to be interoperable with any aircraft, all ANSPs would need to install all the available (certified and operated) links. This is not the case today.
2. the ANSPs are free to install the link(s) of their choice. As a consequence, to be interoperable with any ANSP, all aircraft would need to install all the links implemented (certified and operated) on the ground. This is not the case today.
3. one common link would support the interoperability between all ANSPs and all aircraft. Then, depending on the local constraints and requirements, each ANSP may need to implement additional link (s). As a consequence, depending on the rules defined in the AIP, an aircraft could be required to be equipped with these additional links. 

To support option 3, a common link, such as VDL2 today, is required. More details will be provided in the framework of decision points planned in the CNS EP 2026 and inform any potential change in the strategy to be adopted by the community.

comment.author: IATA

As stated in IATA's CRD on the v0.6, it is important to consider that the current version v3 of the CP2 proposal under consultation includes a requirement for the AU to "contract the A/G subnetwork services directly to the A/G subnetwork CSP".

- CSPs providing the A/G subnetwork services connecting the aircraft with the backbone.

comment.author: Collins Aerospace
comment.comment:
VDLM2 is still actively being built out and we are deploying new ground stations, frequencies and expanding the network globally as well as incorporating network enhancements that use the spectrum more efficiently. Additionally Advanced VHF is in the process of being prototyped and will triple the capacity VHF can provide, while also leveraging the existing VDLM2 architecture and frequencies in use today. It will also allow for a organic cost effective evolution of VHF to meet the growing data demands for the foreseeable future. 

While new technology will be layered into datalink its important to continue the evolution of VHF/VDL which is an essential mission critical, highly reliable system. It's frequency spectrum is 100% dedicated to the global aviation community. VHF is a global solution and as new media come into the profile they will add additional layers of media coverage and capacity but will not replace VDLM2. 

There are still many uncertainties regarding LDACS to confirm both from a network deployment cost and a performance perspective (maturity, compatibility),as reflected in section 2.3 (Milestones and decision points). As the technology matures it will not replace VDL2, but rather supplement it. We dispute the notion of successor to VDL2: both will co-exist if LDACS gets deployed. There is currently no plan to decommission VDL2, but rather several initiatives to expand its capabilities both on VDL2 today and by expanding the capacity through put with Advanced VHF. There are also existing RFI problems that need to be resolved with LDACS before its a viable supplemental solution. Global spectrum allocation for LDACS also needs to be secured.

VDL2 will continue to exist and will benefit from improved performance thanks to the addition of additional frequencies, network enhancements and the introduction of the Advanced-VHF evolution, provided significant additional capacity.
comment.response: The ability of VDL2 enhancements to future advanced ATS services is planned to be assessed in the framework of the CNS Evolution Plans. Section 2.2.2.1, 4th paragraph, provides the definition of successor to VDL2, the reference being SDO 7.3 of the ATM MP 2025. The decision points will provided more detailed assessment as new technologies become mature. Footnote added to precise that the transition is to be defined. No VDL2 decommissioning is foreseen at this stage.
comment.proposedAction: From A/G data communication perspective, by 2035, the deployment of LDACS and Advanced-VHF (A-VHF)  is anticipated to begin to accommodate the expected data traffic growth and meet the  additional performance requirements needed to support future ATS services. Around the same time, HYCON (Hybrid Communication Network) is expected to start deployment as a complementary technology relying on public networks while being supported by safety-of-life links. VDL2 performance will be extended thanks to additional frequencies as well as other enhancements such as regional VGS and ACARS message compression, potentially extended by space-based VHF, Iris SATCOM, additional SATCOM Class B technology, LDACS, and HYCON subnetworks are likely to coexist, necessitating the implementation of a multilink infrastructure

comment.author: Collins Aerospace
comment.comment:
This comment is applicable to the risk register in Appendix B, section 2.1.4, and cross-reference to TBO drivers in Section 2 introduction.

It is recommended that the risk register includes an explicit reference to the lack of a clearly articulated normative mapping between:
 current CP1 capabilities,
 the evolving EU PBN Implementing Rule (including RMT.0761 updates),
 emerging Advanced RNP specifications, and
 the trajectory performance objectives required for CP2 and future TBO implementation.

While CP1 focuses primarily on datalink and surveillance mandates, its performance assumptions rely on consistent airborne trajectory predictability and PBN conformance. The absence of a clear regulatory and performance bridge between CP1 and CP2/TBO risks generating fragmented NAV avionics implementations, including divergent FMS multi-sensor integration approaches across OEM platforms.

Given that the Flight Management System is the integrating airborne element for RNP, RTA performance, ADS-C trajectory prediction, and multi-sensor navigation logic, greater visibility of the airborne/FMS dimension within the roadmap would support system-level coherence and reduce the risk of non-aligned NAV solutions across ECAC.

Without this alignment, there is a risk of:
 inconsistent Advanced RNP implementation,
 uneven RTA performance envelopes,
 suboptimal trajectory predictability for TBO scalability, and
 reduced interoperability across ANSP and aircraft platforms.

Addressing this under CP2 and the EPAS cycle would strengthen the transition toward multi-sensor RNP as the European baseline and ensure alignment between regulatory intent and airborne implementation capability.
comment.response: We approciate the suggestion. This will be addressed in the risk workshop later this year.
comment.proposedAction: Normative Alignment of PBN, CP1, CP2 and TBO

comment.author: Collins Aerospace
comment.comment:
It is recommended that Recommendations 31 and 32, taken together, explicitly address the consolidation and prioritization of the PBN specifications required to support:
 the European multi-sensor RNP baseline,
 Advanced RNP capabilities,
 RTA performance envelopes consistent with TBO objectives, and
 coherent airborne multi-sensor integration (e.g. within flight management systems) across Europe.

While Recommendation 31 clarifies TBO scope and CNS performance requirements, Recommendation 32 should ensure that the regulatory and implementation framework evolves in a synchronized manner through structured input to the EASA EPAS cycle and associated Rulemaking Tasks.

This approach would help prevent divergence between regulatory intent, CNS roadmap milestones, and airborne equipage development cycles, thereby strengthening coherence between CP2 evolution, NAV RON deployment, and long-term TBO performance objectives.
