comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA welcomes the CNS Evolution Plan 2026 as a comprehensive and well-structured document. However, the Plan consistently treats human capacity as a downstream implementation enabler rather than a critical path dependency of equal standing to technology and regulation. Given that virtually every roadmap element in this document  RON deployment, IP migration, cybersecurity hardening, new surveillance data delivery, HAO integration  requires ATSEP expertise for design, commissioning, validation, and ongoing operation, IFATSEA considers that the Plan should state more clearly and early that people are as much a precondition for success as standards and equipment.
comment.response: Ok, a mention of ATSEPs' role will be included in the Exec Summary.
comment.proposedAction: Include an explicit acknowledgement that the successful implementation of all CNS evolution pathways described in this Plan is contingent on adequate ATSEP workforce capacity, competence, and institutional knowledge  not only on technology readiness and funding.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA supports the explicit focus on ATSEP in this edition but is concerned that limiting the scope may understate the workforce challenge. The CNS transformation described in this Plan involves a substantial shift toward software-defined, service-oriented, cloud-hosted systems. The competence profile needed to manage these systems extends significantly beyond the current ATSEP regulatory definition. Limiting the workforce analysis to the existing ATSEP category risks creating a false picture of human capacity adequacy. Future editions should address this gap explicitly.
comment.response: CNS PM will further evaluate this and evenetually would consider this for future versions of the evolution plan.
comment.proposedAction: Strengthen this statement by noting that the narrowing to ATSEP is a deliberate scope decision for this edition, and that a holistic workforce analysis  including engineering, project management, cybersecurity, and data service specialists  is needed in future editions to reflect the full human dimension of CNS evolution.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA has consistently raised the risk of institutional knowledge erosion as ATM systems migrate from hardware-defined to software-defined architectures. The competencies required to manage, troubleshoot, and validate complex virtualised, cloud-hosted CNS services are not the same as those required for current equipment. This Plan correctly identifies 'knowledge transfer' as an element of the ATSEP framework but does not translate this into specific actions or recommendations. A dedicated recommendation on structured knowledge capture and transfer  particularly in the context of vendor dependency and outsourcing  is needed. This aligns directly with IFATSEA's positions as expressed in the CEGHD and in submissions to EU strategy consultations.
comment.response: This will be added.
comment.proposedAction: Add: 'The Plan should explicitly recognise that the transition to software-defined, virtualised CNS infrastructure creates a knowledge transfer risk that is not self-resolving. ANSPs and regulators must invest in structured knowledge retention programmes alongside technology deployment programmes, treating them as co-dependent critical path items.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA considers the risk of over-outsourcing to be one of the most significant structural threats to CNS safety over the period covered by this Plan. The document acknowledges vendor dependency risk but stops short of recommending a minimum internal competence threshold. IFATSEA urges that this Plan explicitly discourage service models in which ANSPs lose the ability to independently assess or validate the systems they operate. This is consistent with positions taken in the IFATSEA submission on the EU Aviation and Aeronautics Strategy and with the principle that safety authority cannot be outsourced.
comment.response: It will be considered and this risk will be added also.
comment.proposedAction: Add a specific statement: 'Outsourcing of CNS system support functions shall not result in loss of safety-critical technical competence within ANSPs. ANSPs must retain sufficient in-house technical authority to independently validate, accept, and certify CNS system behaviour, including during degraded or emergency operating states, regardless of the contracted support model.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA welcomes the inclusion of Training and Continuous Competence as a key human principle. However, the practical application described reflects a reactive model  training aligned to modernisation timelines  rather than a proactive one. Based on IFATSEA members' experience across European ANSPs, the most damaging workforce transitions have been those where technology was deployed before adequate training was in place. This Plan should explicitly recommend that training readiness is a formal gate criterion in the deployment approval process.
comment.response: TBD
comment.proposedAction: Extend this principle to include: 'Training programmes must be designed and updated in pace with CNS technology deployment, not after it. Regulators and ANSPs should establish a formal requirement that ATSEP training curricula are reviewed and updated as a precondition for, not a consequence of, each major CNS system upgrade or transition milestone.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA strongly supports the SUR RON concept and the national planning obligation. However, the Plan does not address a practical operational risk that is well known to ATSEP practitioners: surveillance coverage in European airspace is maintained through cross-border radar data sharing. When adjacent national providers carry out maintenance activities simultaneously  even when individually planned  the resulting coverage degradation can be significant. This is not a theoretical risk; IFATSEA members with operational experience in cross-border environments report that de-confliction of maintenance windows is currently left entirely to bilateral informal arrangements. The SUR RON concept, which explicitly relies on network-level redundancy, cannot function as intended if network-level maintenance coordination is absent. IFATSEA recommends that the Plan assign responsibility for establishing such coordination to EUROCONTROL through its existing AFAG and NM frameworks, and that national RON plans be required to include a cross-border maintenance coordination protocol as a deliverable.
comment.response: Indeed, it is a valid point and is already included in Recommendation 39. Namely, maintenance notification and scheduling coordination should be covered by cross-border CNS service provision SLA. AFAG group is currently working on this as part of its Volume 4 dedicated to CNS.
comment.proposedAction: Add a new recommendation: 'Establish a formal cross-border maintenance coordination mechanism for surveillance infrastructure. States and ANSPs sharing surveillance coverage areas should coordinate planned and unplanned maintenance activities to avoid simultaneous degradation of cross-border surveillance service, and should document this coordination in national SUR RON implementation plans.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: This comment is closely related to the observation on Recommendation 10. Cross-border surveillance coverage is achieved through the aggregation of multiple national sensor networks. The resilience of that network  which is the core premise of the SUR RON concept  can be compromised not only by equipment failure or data-sharing gaps but by uncoordinated maintenance. IFATSEA notes that this issue is specifically relevant for surveillance (where cross-border sensor dependency is operational reality) and for NAVAIDs (where DME-based RON performance similarly depends on network-wide sensor availability). It is less acute for voice communications, which remain primarily sector-oriented. The Plan should acknowledge this asymmetry and address the surveillance and navigation coordination gap explicitly.
comment.response: Indeed, it is a valid point and is already included in Recommendation 39. Namely, maintenance notification and scheduling coordination should be covered by cross-border CNS service provision SLA. AFAG group is currently working on this as part of its Volume 4 dedicated to CNS.
comment.proposedAction: Extend the scope of this challenge paragraph to explicitly include cross-border maintenance coordination as a structural vulnerability: 'In addition to data-sharing and interoperability gaps, the absence of formal coordination mechanisms for planned and unplanned maintenance activities across adjacent surveillance service providers represents a significant operational risk. Overlapping maintenance windows by separate national providers can degrade the cross-border surveillance service that the RON concept depends on. This coordination gap should be addressed as part of the SUR RON governance framework.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA fully supports Recommendation 6 and its inclusion of maintenance and workforce sustainability as plan components. The addition of cross-border coordination for NAVAID maintenance is the navigation-domain equivalent of the observation made in relation to SUR RON: the NAV RON concept relies on network performance that is jointly produced by multiple national providers. A DME network node maintained by State A contributes to the RON performance experienced in State B's airspace. Uncoordinated maintenance in one State can silently degrade another State's RON performance below design parameters. This is precisely the type of cross-domain, cross-border risk that a plan of this scope should address. IFATSEA notes that coordination mechanisms for NAVAID maintenance are significantly less developed than those for ATC operations, and urges that this gap be explicitly recognised in the Plan.
comment.response:
Following sentence added with minor modification: "In addition, national NAV RON implementation plans should address coordination regarding planned maintenance activities and operational status notifications of shared cross-border NAVAIDs in order to protect the network-level performance of the NAV RON. "
Further details will be clarified in CNS RON concept deliverable.
comment.proposedAction: Extend the planning obligation to include: 'National NAV RON implementation plans should include a cross-border coordination protocol defining how adjacent NAVAID service providers will de-conflict planned maintenance activities, share performance degradation notifications, and coordinate unplanned outage responses in order to protect the network-level performance of the NAV RON.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: Such agreements should also define common arrangements for flight inspection activities in accordance with ICAO Doc 8071 standards. Importantly, these flight inspections should assess the NAVAID throughout its full operational coverage area, ensuring that validation extends beyond national airspace and encompasses the complete service volume effectively used by aircraft. This should, naturally, be conducted under appropriate diplomatic, operational, and technical coordination, including coordinated and authorised flight inspection profiles.
comment.response:
Proposed text introduces specific details of cross-border cooperation and operational arrangements. Those better fit in Appendix D on NAV RON concept and design criteria. 
Text was modified and added to Appendix D.
Further details will be clarified in CNS RON concept deliverable.
comment.proposedAction: Navigational infrastructure used across borders requires enhanced coordination mechanisms. While published procedures may reference sovereign NAVAIDs, the transmitted signals are routinely utilized by avionics operating across international boundaries. For this reason, the establishment of formal Service Level Agreements (SLAs) between neighbouring States is strongly recommended to ensure the reliability, continuity, and coordinated management of navigation services.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: Furthermore, although the document provides a relatively detailed description of potential operational and technical weaknesses and their possible impacts, the proposed mitigation measures remain overly generic and lack concrete implementation mechanisms, governance structures, responsibilities, timelines, or measurable deliverables. As currently presented, many of the mitigation actions appear more aspirational than operationally actionable.
comment.response: In terms of managing any risk to the EP, the risk register in the current draft is a place holder for a more robust risk assessment workshop which will be held in Q4 2026. The risks and mitigations related to any element of the EP will therefore be elaborated at the risk workshop, taking account of the relevant stakeholder experience and expertise.
comment.proposedAction:
It is therefore recommended that the document be strengthened through the inclusion of:                                                                                                                                  1) clearly defined mitigation strategies and implementation roadmaps,
2) identified responsible actors and coordination mechanisms,
3) measurable performance and safety indicators,
4) realistic implementation timelines,
5) contingency and fallback arrangements,
and concrete governance provisions to ensure monitoring, accountability, and effective risk management throughout the deployment lifecycle.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA has consistently argued  in CEGHD submissions, in its EU Aviation Strategy comments, and in technical working groups  that the separation of CNS cybersecurity from operational safety management creates a dangerous governance gap. The Plan correctly identifies cybersecurity as a strategic risk but does not resolve the question of who is responsible for managing cyber threats at the operational level. IFATSEA urges that this Plan explicitly assign ATSEP a formal role in operational cybersecurity and that this be reflected in the regulatory framework update recommended elsewhere in the document.
comment.response: Assigning responsibility for managing cyber security should be left to the relevant regulations and authorities. Future EP will take account of any development in this regard. In terms of managing any risk to cyber security, the risk register in the current draft is a place holder for a more robust risk assessment workshop whihc will be held in Q4 2026. The risks and mitigations related to cyber security should therefore be elaborated at the risk workshop, taking account of the relevant stakeholder experience and expertise.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: GNSS jamming and spoofing threats may be significantly mitigated through the progressive deployment of DFMC (Dual Frequency Multi-Constellation) GNSS capabilities, provided that both airborne and ground systems are appropriately equipped and supported by the necessary regulatory and certification framework. In particular, aircraft equipage capable of processing multiple constellations and dual frequencies (e.g. L1 and L5) would considerably enhance navigation resilience, signal integrity, continuity, and resistance to interference and spoofing events.n parallel, the wider implementation of space-based surveillance technologies, including space-based Wide Area Multilateration (WAM), could substantially contribute to the establishment of a more resilient and geographically independent surveillance environment within the future Resilient Operational Network (RON) architecture. Such capabilities may provide additional redundancy, improved surveillance continuity, and enhanced operational resilience during GNSS degradation or disruption scenarios.
comment.response:
1. EP supports progressive deployment of DFMC: please see paragraphs (251) and recommendation 8.
2. EP calls for harmonisation of equippage and regulatory actions: please see Recommendation 17
3. the need for coordination among various stakeholders, in multiple areas, is frequently mentioned numerous sections of the EP and is also included in the risk management activities.
4. Spectrum protection needs and efforts are addressed thoroughly in section 2.2.5 and recommendations in section 3.1.6. Space-based WAM as independent SUR means has been elaborated extensively in the EP, including mentioning potential benefits it could bring to resilience and ground infrastructure optimisation (if its performance proves to be adequate).
4.
comment.proposedAction:
It is therefore recommended that the evolution plan: 1) explicitly support the phased introduction of DFMC capabilities across both airborne and ground infrastructures,
2) promote harmonised regulatory and equipage strategies,
3) include coordinated resilience validation and operational testing,
4) assess interoperability and spectrum protection requirements,
and further evaluate the operational role of space-based surveillance and WAM technologies as part of the future RON framework and contingency architecture.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The Plan's cybersecurity recommendation is scoped only to communications infrastructure. IFATSEA considers this scope insufficient given the documented growth in GNSS jamming and spoofing events affecting both navigation and surveillance simultaneously, and the introduction of IP-based connectivity in surveillance data delivery systems. A holistic CNS cybersecurity assessment is consistent with IFATSEA's position that cybersecurity is an operational safety function spanning all CNS domains.
comment.response: Noted. The point is relevant and valid. However,the risk register and risk management regime for the CNS EP exists to support the successful execution of the Evolution plan. See also annswer to #286.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA considers Recommendation 44 to be the most operationally important recommendation in the entire Plan. It is, however, the least specific: it calls for a review without a deadline, without defined scope boundaries, and without a clear regulatory output obligation. IFATSEA urges that this recommendation be strengthened materially. The competency gap between current ATSEP training frameworks and the skills required to manage the CNS systems described in this Plan is real, documented, and growing. Specifically: (i) cybersecurity operational competence is not addressed in Reg 2017/373; (ii) AI-assisted system oversight is not addressed; (iii) cloud and virtualised system management requires a fundamentally different technical knowledge base from current equipment-centred training. IFATSEA commits to contributing actively to the competency review and urges that professional staff associations be included as mandatory consultees, not optional stakeholders.
comment.response:
Noted. Suggested update: 

The evolution towards increasingly digital, software-based and automated CNS architectures introduces a number of questions regarding the future roles, competencies and interfaces associated with ATSEP functions. Such questions extend beyond technology and encompass human performance, resilience, knowledge retention, lifecycle management and interaction with external suppliers and service providers. While these topics are recognised as important elements of the long-term evolution of CNS, the 2026 CNS Evolution Plan does not seek to prejudge future regulatory, organisational or professional arrangements. Rather, these areas are considered subjects for continuing evolution whose treatment should be informed by operational experience, technological maturity, safety evidence and broad stakeholder engagement.

In line with the living nature of the CNS Evolution Plan, uncertainties associated with the evolution of ATSEP roles and responsibilities should be identified and monitored through the Plan's associated risk management process. Human capability, competence evolution, knowledge continuity and organisational resilience may be considered alongside technical and regulatory risks, allowing emerging issues to be identified and assessed in a timely manner. Future editions of the Plan may refine or expand this treatment where supported by evidence and stakeholder consensus.

To support this work, it is recommended that the <<Insert ATSEP work group here>> consider establishing a subgroup to examine the evolution of ATSEP roles and associated competencies in the context of CNS transformation over the period 2026-2050, ensuring sufficient engagement with stakeholders and regulatory authorities. Such work could consider, inter alia, the implications of software-defined infrastructures, increasing automation, artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, external service provision, knowledge retention, human performance and resilience. The outputs of this activity sould provide an input to the preparation of the 2028 CNS Evolution Plan, helping ensure that future revisions continue to reflect both technological developments and the human capabilities required to safely and efficiently support them.
comment.proposedAction: explicitly support the phased introduction of DFMC capabilities across both airborne and ground infrastructures,

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA welcomes the inclusion of ACE workforce data in the Plan. However, citing a current headcount figure without projecting future demand is insufficient for a plan running to 2050. Based on IFATSEA members' national reports, demographic pressures  retirements, difficulty recruiting technically qualified candidates, and the lengthening time-to-competency for new ATSEP  are already creating operational pressure in several European ANSPs. The CNS transition programme described in this Plan will substantially increase demand for ATSEP expertise during exactly the period when workforce numbers are under the greatest demographic pressure. This mismatch should be explicitly identified and addressed. IFATSEA is prepared to contribute workforce projection data from its member associations to support this analysis.
comment.response: Noted. See response to #289
comment.proposedAction: promote harmonised regulatory and equipage strategies,

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA is deeply concerned by the governance gap in the ADSP-based surveillance delivery model. When surveillance data is processed and delivered by an external service provider, it becomes unclear which ATSEP  within which organisation  holds operational responsibility for data quality validation, system performance monitoring, and degraded-mode response. The Plan acknowledges business model and economic challenges but does not address the safety authority question. IFATSEA considers this to be a critical omission: the accountability for surveillance data integrity is a safety-critical question that must be resolved before, not after, deployment. This concern is consistent with IFATSEA's broader position that safety authority and operational competence must be retained within ANSPs and must not be implicitly outsourced through service delivery model transitions.
comment.response: Paragraph (167) already recognises that certain governance aspects remain to be addressed. As per paragraph (165) , CNS service provision, be it by historical or new delivery model, remains subject to 2017/373 provisions, which require among other elements, thorough safety assessment and certification. Examples of certified pan-European CNS service provision successfully relying on remote data and contribution of remote ATSEP already exist.
comment.proposedAction: include coordinated resilience validation and operational testing,

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The Plan's closing section identifies governance, coordination, spectrum, and technology as binding conditions for Vision realisation. It does not identify workforce capacity as an equivalent binding condition, despite the evidence presented in Section 2.2.8 and the breadth of competency requirements implied by the deployment programme. IFATSEA urges that human capacity  specifically ATSEP workforce adequacy  be listed alongside technology maturity, governance, and spectrum protection as a structural precondition for achieving the Vision.
comment.response: Ok, this will be mentioned.
comment.proposedAction: assess interoperability and spectrum protection requirements,

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA notes that the Plan's environmental section addresses energy consumption and carbon footprint comprehensively. The concept of sustainability, however, also applies to human resources. Long-term CNS viability depends on the ability to attract, train, and retain technically competent ATSEP over the 25-year horizon of this Plan. This is a sustainability challenge of equal strategic significance to energy efficiency. IFATSEA does not suggest displacing the environmental content but urges that the Plan acknowledge human resource sustainability as part of its overall long-term viability framework.
comment.response: Noted. These topics are covered in the Human aspects chapter.
comment.proposedAction: and further evaluate the operational role of space-based surveillance and WAM technologies as part of the future RON framework and contingency architecture.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA's ATSEP Safety Study (2024) presents the full quantified safety evidence base for the safety criticality of ATSEP roles, drawing on the EUROCONTROL AIM and the ECORYS/NLR study commissioned by EASA in 2013. The CNS Evolution Plan 2026 references ATSEP competence as important but does not cite the available quantitative safety evidence. The Plan's credibility  and its ability to drive regulatory and investment decisions  would be significantly strengthened by explicitly grounding the human dimension section in this evidence. Five specific ATSEP job categories (ATSEP-SMC, ATSEP-SUR, ATSEP-NAV, ATSEP-COM, ATSEP-DP) were identified as safety-critical in the ECORYS/NLR study, corresponding exactly to the CNS domains covered by this Plan. The Plan should reference this study directly.
comment.response: Ok, the  EASA-commissioned ECORYS/NLR study will be checked,  the text amended and a reference added as necessary. Further study can be carried out as per reply to comment #289, and outcome considered for CNS EP 2028,
comment.proposedAction: Supplement Recommendation 44 with an explicit reference to the quantified safety evidence base. The Plan should state: 'The safety criticality of ATSEP roles has been formally confirmed by the EASA-commissioned ECORYS/NLR study (2013), which found that ATSEP tasks contribute to the last safety barrier in the ATM system across five accident scenarios. Based on the EUROCONTROL Accident Incident Model (AIM), failure of ATSEP performance increases mid-air collision probability by +17%, runway incursion probability by +15%, taxiway collision probability by +10%, and wake-induced accident probability by +17%. Failure of the ATSEP-maintained Ground Proximity Warning barrier increases CFIT accident probability by a factor of 50. These statistics provide the safety case foundation for the workforce and competency recommendations in this Plan.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA has advocated for inclusion of ATSEP in ICAO Annex 1 since 2004, submitting Working Papers at every ICAO General Assembly since A35 (2004). The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) presents the comprehensive safety, regulatory, and economic case. The CNS Evolution Plan spans to 2050  a timeline that fully intersects with the ICAO Annex 1 amendment process. If the decision is not taken during the current regulatory cycle, the CNS transition described in this Plan will proceed without the globally harmonised ATSEP licensing framework that would ensure consistent competency across all European and international ANSPs contributing to the ECAC network. IFATSEA notes that national ATSEP licensing frameworks already exist in Germany, Turkey, Greece, Croatia, Czech Republic, Serbia, Bulgaria, France, Montenegro, Kazakhstan and others  demonstrating that the regulatory groundwork is in place and that a global standard is both achievable and needed.
comment.response: While the Evolution plan recognises the challenges relating to ATSEP competence and skill requirements going forward, operational licencing is outside of the scope of the CNS Evolution plan
comment.proposedAction: Add a dedicated sub-recommendation under Recommendation 44: 'Support the inclusion of ATSEP in ICAO Annex 1 for Personnel Licensing. ATSEP is currently the only safety-critical aviation profession included in ICAO's NGAP (Next Generation Aviation Professionals) programme with established training and competency frameworks (ICAO Doc 10057) that is not yet covered by ICAO Annex 1 licensing. Given that ICAO Annex 1 amendment cycles take up to 8 years, a decision to proceed must be taken without further delay if the licensing framework is to be in place before the peak of the CNS transition described in this Plan.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA has formally proposed this KPI to EASA, EUROCONTROL, and IATA, and it is included in IFATSEA's submission to the EU Aviation and Aeronautics Strategy consultation (2026). The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) documents a trend of rising CNS/ATM system outages between 2010 and 2024, with economic losses in the hundreds of millions of Euros per event, significant environmental impact from rerouting, and direct safety implications. The Plan currently lacks a network-level KPI for CNS infrastructure availability and reliability. Recommendation 11 addresses interrogation reduction but does not address outage monitoring across domains. IFATSEA considers this a material gap: the Plan advocates for a Resilient Operational Network but provides no mechanism to measure whether that network is actually performing resiliently. A standardised outage KPI would provide the accountability mechanism the RON concept requires, link CNS performance to the Performance and Charging Scheme, and enable systematic root cause analysis across the five CNS domains.
comment.response: Thank you for this constructive proposal. Although a number of CNS monitoring KPIs already exist and are closely monitored daily by the NM, this new proposal will be examined internally. Bearing in mind the timeframe of proposal assessment and the schedule of the EP 2026 consultation, a potential EP update could be realistically envisaged for the EP 2028 edition at the earliest.
comment.proposedAction: Introduce a dedicated CNS/ATM Outages and Degradations KPI into the Plan's performance monitoring architecture: Name: CNS/ATM Outages & Degradations. Calculation: (Number of qualifying outages or degradations / Total IFR flight hours) × 100,000. Qualifying event: Unplanned loss or degradation of surveillance, navigation, communication, automation, or data processing lasting ?15 minutes, or causing ATC capacity reduction, route unavailability, or significant delays. Target: 50% reduction in outage rate by 2030 (baseline: 2024). Monitoring scope: all five CNS domains plus cybersecurity-related unavailability. Roadmap: define and baseline 20262027; deploy dashboard and root cause analysis 20272028; reduce event rate 20282029; achieve target and institutionalise 20292031.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA's EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) establishes Human-in-Command as a core principle and states that mandatory human oversight for safety-critical aviation functions is non-negotiable. This is not a constraint on AI deployment  it is a precondition for safe AI deployment in a safety-critical environment. The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) identifies ATSEP cybersecurity and AI oversight as an emerging first-responder function: ATSEP must be able to distinguish whether a CNS system anomaly is a technical failure or a cyber/AI event, and must have the tools, training, and formal authority to intervene. The CNS Evolution Plan describes a trajectory toward increasingly AI-assisted CNS management but does not specify what human oversight mechanisms are required. IFATSEA urges that each AI-related deployment recommendation in the Plan be accompanied by a corresponding human oversight specification, and that ATSEP be named as the designated oversight function.
comment.response: The EP does not assign operational responsibilities. We note the points raised, and the risks relating to AI deployment in the context of the Evolution plan  should be assessed at the risk workshop in Q4 2026. However, the assignment of operational responsibilities is outside the scpe of the evolution plan and rests with the competent authorities and operational service providers, under the relevant regulations.
comment.proposedAction: Strengthen the AI-related human factors principle by adding: 'The Plan should explicitly adopt Human-in-Command principles for all AI-enabled CNS functions. ATSEP professionals must hold defined, formal roles in the validation, monitoring, technical supervision, and operational assurance of AI-assisted CNS systems. Operational explainability of AI outputs  meaning that ATSEP can understand, verify, and if necessary override AI-generated system assessments  must be a mandatory design requirement for all AI-assisted CNS equipment deployed under this Plan.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA's EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) and the ATSEP Safety Study (2024) both address the risk of over-outsourcing as a strategic threat to CNS safety. The Safety Study notes that ATSEP numbers and CNS expenditure are falling while system outages are rising  a correlation that reflects the consequences of reduced internal technical capacity. The Plan's ADSP model accelerates this trend by moving CNS service provision to external providers. IFATSEA does not oppose service-oriented architectures but insists that safety authority  the ability to independently assess CNS system behaviour, validate performance claims, and respond to degraded-mode events  must remain within ANSPs. Europe's aviation resilience depends on strategic autonomy in technical expertise, not only in hardware supply chains. This is consistent with the EU's broader strategic autonomy objectives and should be reflected explicitly in the Plan's governance recommendations.
comment.response: Noted. See #289. The risks and mitigations related to the EP will be elaborated at the risk workshop in Q4 2026, taking account of the relevant stakeholder experience and expertise.
comment.proposedAction: Add a governance safeguard to the ADSP model: 'The transition to service-oriented CNS delivery must not result in the erosion of sovereign technical expertise within ANSPs. The Plan should recommend that States and ANSPs define and maintain a minimum internal technical competence threshold  sufficient to independently validate, certify, and supervise externally delivered CNS services  as a regulatory requirement. Outsourcing of CNS service management shall not constitute outsourcing of safety authority. Excessive fragmentation of responsibilities, creation of single points of external dependency, and loss of internal technical knowledge must be actively discouraged through governance design and regulatory requirement.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) documents a rising shortage of ATSEP  identified by both EUROCONTROL and IFATSEA  as young engineers increasingly find the profession unattractive due to demanding 24/7 rostering, high responsibility, and insufficient recognition, compounded by the profession's exclusion from ICAO Annex 1. IFATSEA national reports from the 2026 Euro Conference record staff shortages in Albania, Norway, Slovakia, Spain, Switzerland, Türkiye, Belgium, Hungary and others  representing a systemic rather than isolated workforce problem. The ACE 2025 figure of 8,724 FTE captures only the current state. The CNS deployment milestones in Sections 2.2 and 2.3  NAV RON by 2030, SUR RON by 2040, full ADSP-based surveillance delivery, HAO CNS infrastructure, LDACS deployment  each require significant ATSEP expertise in parallel. Without a forward workforce model, the Plan risks setting milestones that cannot be met for human capacity reasons rather than technology or funding reasons.
comment.response: The risks associated with timely delivery of milestones is noted. These should be elaborated and mitigated as part of the risk workshop in Q2 2026
comment.proposedAction: Commission a forward-looking ATSEP workforce adequacy assessment as a Plan deliverable: 'By 2027, EUROCONTROL in collaboration with IFATSEA and ANSPs should produce a quantitative assessment of ATSEP workforce adequacy relative to the CNS transition programme, projecting FTE requirements by domain and deployment phase, identifying demographic pressure points and recruitment pipeline gaps, and recommending minimum workforce investment levels to ensure that human capacity does not become the critical path constraint on CNS evolution milestones.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The ATSEP Safety Study (2024, Chapter 14.1) establishes that ATSEP are the appropriate first responders for ATM/ANS cybersecurity events, as they are uniquely positioned to distinguish whether a CNS anomaly is a technical failure or a cyber attack  a distinction that has immediate safety consequences. The attack scenario described in the Study  network attack combined with GNSS spoofing/jamming to create service disruption or safety degradation  is exactly the compound threat environment addressed in the Plan's surveillance and navigation chapters. The EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) states: 'Cybersecurity in aviation is an issue which can directly jeopardize safety and consequently it has to be managed by personnel such as ATSEP who are able to assess the impact to ATM/ANS networks of any imminent threat.' The Plan identifies cybersecurity as a risk but does not assign operational responsibility. This gap must be closed.
comment.response: The EP does not assign operational responsibilities. While the points raised are relevant, the assignment of operational responsibilities rests with the competent authorities and operational service providers, under the relevant regulations.
comment.proposedAction: Formalise the ATSEP cybersecurity first-responder role: 'The Plan should recommend that ANSPs formally designate ATSEP as the operational cybersecurity first responders for CNS/ATM systems. This designation should include: (a) a defined response protocol distinguishing technical failure from cyber event; (b) ATSEP training in CNS-specific cybersecurity threat identification, triage, and response; (c) ATSEP inclusion in national and European cybersecurity coordination structures for aviation; and (d) revision of Regulation (EU) 2017/373 to include cybersecurity response as an explicit ATSEP competency domain.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA's EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) calls explicitly for updating Regulation (EU) 2017/373 to include appropriate training streams for AI oversight, cybersecurity, cloud services, SOA, ATM Data Services, virtualisation, remote operations, and cyber resilience. The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) identifies global standardisation gaps in ATSEP training and notes that ICAO Doc 10057 training provisions remain subject to individual State discretion, creating inconsistent competency levels across the ECAC network. The CNS Evolution Plan describes a transition that fundamentally changes the technical environment in which ATSEP operate  from hardware-maintained, equipment-centred systems to software-defined, network-centric, AI-assisted service architectures. The current ATSEP competency framework was designed for the former environment. Without a regulatory update, ANSPs and regulators will be deploying new CNS systems against a competency standard that does not address the skills required to manage them.
comment.response: Noted. The points raised are valid, but any changes to EU 2017/373 is outside the scope of the CNS EP 2026 itself. However, the risk workshop in Q4 2026 should examine ATSEP competence related risks and derive appropriate mitigations. Further actions are detailed in #289
comment.proposedAction: Recommend an urgent update of Regulation (EU) 2017/373 to add explicit training streams for: (a) cybersecurity operational response; (b) AI-assisted system oversight and explainability; (c) cloud-based and virtualised CNS system management; (d) software-defined infrastructure operation; and (e) service-oriented architecture and ATM Data Services. The update should be timed to align with the CNS deployment milestones in Section 2.3, ensuring that regulatory competency requirements precede, rather than follow, technology deployment.

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: IFATSEA's EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) states this principle explicitly. The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) demonstrates through case studies  Ueberlingen, Linate, Swanwick  that governance failures at the CNS management level have direct safety consequences. The CNS Evolution Plan's governance section focuses on inter-institutional coordination (EUROCONTROL, EASA, NM, States) but does not include operational technical personnel as a formal governance layer. IFATSEA considers this a gap: the Plan's transition depends on ATSEP expertise for delivery, but ATSEP are not currently named as participants in the governance frameworks described. This should be corrected.
comment.response: It is the intention of CNS PM to work with the governance body to ensure  sufficient representation of stakeholders in the governance of risk. The exact composition of the governance body is yet to be decided, but it will be based on the existing Institutional Coordination arrangement.
comment.proposedAction: Add to the governance section: 'Future CNS governance structures  including those managing RON implementation, ADSP deployment, cybersecurity coordination, and AI integration  should systematically include ATSEP representation. ATSEP professionals directly responsible for operational CNS systems possess the expertise essential for realistic deployment planning, maintainability assessments, operational risk evaluation, and resilience planning. Excluding them from governance structures produces plans disconnected from operational reality.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The IFATSEA EU Aviation Strategy submission (2026) addresses HAO and HAPS explicitly, noting: 'HAO will fly at new altitudes that may incur new security and cyber-security risks... Many HAO will be unmanned, with digital C2 links, increasing their vulnerability to cyber-threats.' This availability can be ensured through highly competent ATSEP who must be extensively trained in new space communication and surveillance systems. The ATSEP Safety Study (2024) identifies cybersecurity as an ATSEP first-responder function. The CNS Evolution Plan describes HAO as an emerging challenge requiring new CNS infrastructure but does not address the ATSEP workforce implications of managing these new systems. Space-based surveillance, HAPS-related communications, and digital C2 link management represent entirely new technical domains for ATSEP. Training preparation should be a Plan deliverable, not a residual implementation task.
comment.response:
To highlight the specific context and novelty of HAO, added a recommendation on the need for ensuring that ATSEP training scope remains adequate.
However, please note that as per the existing regulation and already for the existing ATM/CNS systems, training creation and/or update, both one-off and recurrent, as well as satisfactory test results after the training are already prerequisite and a part of deployment preparation activities for every relevant system change.
It is therefore understood that deployment of any new system intended for certified ATM/CNS service provision would automatically include ATSEP training.
Added a recommendation for ATSEP training for new CNS systems, including those used for managing HAO, before their respective deployment.
comment.proposedAction: Add a specific workforce action to the HAO recommendations: 'ATSEP training frameworks must be extended to include space-based CNS systems, HAPS, digital C2 links, and the cybersecurity-specific threats associated with higher airspace operations. The EASA HAO roadmap explicitly identifies heightened cyber-security risks for HAO operations  ATSEP must be trained and authorised to manage these systems before they become operational, not after.'

comment.author: IFATSEA
comment.comment: The ATSEP Safety Study (2024, Chapter 4.5 and Chapter 12) identifies fatigue and stress as human factors that directly impact ATSEP performance quality, with potential negative safety implications in system monitoring and control. The ECORYS/NLR study (2013) also highlighted fatigue and stress as relevant factors. The CNS Evolution Plan's human dimension table identifies key human factors principles but does not include fatigue management. Given the 24/7 operational nature of ATSEP roles, the increasing number of parallel project demands documented in national reports (Hungary, Serbia, Slovakia, Czech Republic), and the recognised relationship between fatigue and CNS system management quality, IFATSEA considers fatigue management an essential component of the human dimension framework. IFATSEA is working on ATSEP fatigue management guidelines and would contribute to a European framework under this Plan.
comment.response: We note the work IFATSEA and others are doing on this matter. However, fatigue management is an operational matter, and while the concern is valid and should be addressed through the relevant working arrangements, it is not currently in scope of the Human dimension of the CNS Evolution plan.
comment.proposedAction: Add a fatigue and working conditions principle to Table 2: 'Fatigue Management: ATSEP operate in safety-critical roles on 24/7 shift patterns, often at remote or geographically challenging sites. The Plan should recommend development of ATSEP-specific fatigue management guidelines, aligned with the work underway in the ICAO APAC region, and require ANSPs to address shift work design, rest period adequacy, and occupational health as explicit components of workforce sustainability planning.'

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment:
The CNS Evolution Plan assumes that the PBN IR has already been amended and is applicable, and that ILS CAT I falls outside the scope of the regulation. However, this is not the case at present. Until a final proposal from EASA RMT.0761 is adopted and reflected in the regulation, the CNS Evolution Plan should remain aligned with the current applicable EU regulatory framework. 

From EUSPAs perspective, the priority is to ensure that the CNS Evolution Plan remains fully aligned with the current applicable regulatory framework. While it is understood that this framework will evolve in the future (particularly in light of the ongoing work under EASA RMT.0761 by 2028) any such changes should only be reflected once the ED Decision is published. 

In the meantime, if the document includes statements (use of ILS CAT I) that are not aligned with the current regulatory framework, this should be explicitly and clearly stated at the beginning of the document. A simple footnote (footnote 11) is not considered as sufficient to address this misalignment. 

Please consider the possible differentiation between CAT I and CAT II/III operations. We propose to modify as following: "For precision approach and landing operations, ILS, SBAS and GBAS will be used as primary means of navigation for CAT I operations, while ILS and GBAS will be used to support CAT II/III operations". 

Consider this comment along the whole document, where the statement "ILS remains the primary means supporting CAT I/II/III operationsILS CAT I/II/III" and reference to footnote 11 is included .
comment.response:
modify text as proposed: For precision approach and landing operations, ILS, SBAS and GBAS will be used as primary means of navigation for CAT I operations, while ILS and GBAS will be used to support CAT II/III operations. 
add clarification: In the long-term evolution SBAS (LPV200) and GBAS could enable CAT I operations. nowadays no equivalance between ILS and SBAS for CAT I operations.
other option is not to link with operational category.
comment.proposedAction:
Clarification in the text is needed -->CNS EP is not fully consistent with current PBN IR.

For precision approach and landing operations, ILS, SBAS and GBAS will be used as primary means of navigation for CAT I operations.

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment: The document does not also appear to be consistent with the current European ATM Master Plan, in particular SDO9.3 where a rationalization of ILS is proposed while CNS Evolution Plan propose the opposite (Rationalise instrument landing systems (ILS) and implement efficiency measures/methods for more cost-effective maintenance of ILS, providing a link between ICAO Doc. 8071 and national CNS provision).
comment.response: Inconsistency will be clarified between SDO9 and CNS Evolution Plan
comment.proposedAction: Clarification in the text is needed -->CNS EP is not fully consistent with European Master Plan in relation to SDO9.3.

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment: Statement contains several assumptions. PBN IR will be updated and ED Decision (with final proposed text) will not be available until 2028.
comment.response: Text to be updated inline with RMT Terms of Reference
comment.proposedAction: By 2028, an ED Decision with an updated PBN IR (based on the work performed under EASA RMT.0761) will be published.

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment: The inclusion of SBAS (L1) in the Navigation milestones and decision points 2026-2032 is well apprecipated. However, the rationale is not clear enough in the document. During CNS PM Facilitating Workshop (2) it was clarified that this is linked to current reporting activities on the fleet (based on Eurocontrol CNS Dashboard). However, it is proposed to include this readiness by 2030 (instead of 2027) linked to the transition to PBN operations by 2030 as per current PBN IR.
comment.response: Milestone will be moved to 2030 and escription will be modified.
comment.proposedAction: Include a decision/checking point in 2030 (instead of 2027), linked to PBN IR application

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment:
A DFMC equipment (ED-259B) might be considered as a possible safety measure to enhance resilience against GNSS radio-frequency interference (RFI), based on the positive results obtained in the Jammertest 2025. Then, a future mandate on DFMC cannot be ruled out. For this reason, the following wording is proposed:
"Exploring financial incentive mechanisms to encourage voluntary adoption and avoiding the regional mandating of specific GNSS constellations or SBAS in favour of promoting of DFMC GNSS as an enabling capability within a global framework".
comment.response: text will be modified as suggested

comment.author: EUSPA
comment.comment:
Linked to EUSPA comment#1 and #2, this statement is not aligned with SDO9.3 where a rationalization of ILS is proposed while CNS Evolution Plan propose the opposite (Rationalise instrument landing systems (ILS) and implement efficiency measures/methods for more cost-effective maintenance of ILS, providing a link between ICAO Doc. 8071 and national CNS provision).
As mentioned, we propose to modify as following: "For precision approach and landing operations, ILS, SBAS and GBAS will be used as primary means of navigation for CAT I operations, while ILS and GBAS will be used to support CAT II/III operations". 


Please ammend the document accordingly to previous comments resolutions.
comment.response: see comment response 305
